grammar.label: Word order
grammar.description: The verb must appear in the second position in a Norwegian main clause (V2 rule). Errors occur when the subject-verb order is not inverted after a fronted adverbial, or when the basic SVO pattern is broken.

examples.note: After the time adverbial 'i dag', the verb must come before the subject.
Adverbs of frequency sit after the verb in main clauses.

grammar.label: Verb tense
grammar.description: Using the wrong tense for the situation — for example using present tense when past tense is required, or confusing preterite with present perfect.

examples.note: 'I går' (yesterday) requires past tense.
Present perfect requires the past participle, not the infinitive.

grammar.label: Verb conjugation
grammar.description: Using the wrong verb form for the tense or context — e.g. using the infinitive instead of the present tense form, or an incorrect preterite ending.

examples.note: The infinitive marker 'å' is not used in conjugated verb positions.

examples.wrong: Vi snakket ikke norsk i går.
examples.correct: Vi snakket ikke norsk i går.
examples.note: This is actually correct — group 1 verb. Error would be 'Vi snakte'.

grammar.label: Noun gender
grammar.description: Assigning the wrong grammatical gender (en, ei, et) to a noun. Gender affects article choice, definite suffix, adjective agreement, and pronoun reference.

examples.note: 'Bok' is feminine (ei) in Nynorsk, but also accepted as 'en bok' in Bokmål.
'Hus' is a neuter noun.

grammar.label: Article use
grammar.description: Using the wrong indefinite article (en/ei/et) or the wrong definite suffix (-en/-a/-et/-ene/-ene/-ene), or confusing definite and indefinite forms.

examples.note: 'Mann' is a common gender (en) noun.

examples.wrong: boken er stor (about a neuter noun)
examples.note: Neuter nouns take -et in definite singular.

grammar.label: Adjective agreement
grammar.description: The adjective does not agree with the noun it modifies in gender or definiteness. Neuter nouns require -t in indefinite singular; definite and plural forms require -e.

examples.note: Adjectives add -t before indefinite neuter nouns.
Definite constructions require the -e form of the adjective.

grammar.label: Pronoun choice
grammar.description: Using the wrong personal, possessive, or demonstrative pronoun — for example 'den' vs 'det' depending on noun gender, or wrong subject/object form.

examples.note: Possessives typically follow the noun in Norwegian.
Object form of 'hun' is 'henne'.

grammar.label: Preposition
grammar.description: Using the wrong preposition. Norwegian prepositions do not map one-to-one with English, particularly 'i', 'på', 'til', 'av', and 'for'.

examples.note: 'I' is used for countries and enclosed spaces.

examples.wrong: Jeg går til skolen på bussen.
examples.correct: Jeg går til skolen med bussen.
examples.note: 'Med' expresses means of transport.

grammar.label: Modal verb
grammar.description: Misusing a modal verb — wrong modal for the context, or placing 'å' before the following infinitive, which is incorrect with modals.

examples.note: Modal verbs are not followed by 'å' before the infinitive.

examples.wrong: Du må ikke gå dit hvis du vil.
examples.correct: Du trenger ikke gå dit hvis du vil.
examples.note: 'Må ikke' means 'must not' (prohibition), not 'don't have to'.

grammar.label: Negation placement
grammar.description: Placing 'ikke' in the wrong position. In main clauses it follows the verb; in subordinate clauses it precedes the verb.

examples.note: In main clauses, 'ikke' comes after the finite verb.

examples.wrong: Han sa at han liker ikke kaffe.
examples.correct: Han sa at han ikke liker kaffe.
examples.note: In subordinate clauses introduced by 'at', 'ikke' precedes the verb.

grammar.label: Compound word
grammar.description: Writing a compound word as two separate words instead of one, or forming the compound incorrectly. Norwegian compounds are written as single words.

examples.note: Compounds are always written as one word in Norwegian.
The English calque 'bus stop' does not carry over; the Norwegian compound is used.

vocabulary.label: Wrong word (same category)
vocabulary.description: The word chosen is in the same semantic category as the correct word but is not appropriate in context — a false friend, near-synonym, or register mismatch.

examples.note: 'Full' means drunk in Norwegian; 'mett' means full (after eating).
'Fin' means nice/fine; 'vakker' means beautiful.

vocabulary.label: Wrong word (different category)
vocabulary.description: The word used is from a completely different semantic field and does not fit the meaning at all — the word is simply not known.

examples.wrong: Jeg vil kjøpe en stol til middag.
examples.note: 'Kjøpe en stol' (buy a chair) is semantically unrelated to dinner preparation.

vocabulary.label: Spelling
vocabulary.description: The correct word is used but spelled incorrectly — including misspelled Norwegian letters (æ, ø, å), double consonants, or English spelling imported into Norwegian.

examples.note: The Norwegian first-person pronoun is 'jeg', not 'jæg'.
This is correct — a common confusion is 'gata' (definite) vs 'gate' (indefinite).
Example of æøå confusion: 'grøn' vs correct 'grønn' (double n).

comprehension.label: Listening recognition
comprehension.description: The learner cannot parse the spoken audio correctly — mishearing words, missing unstressed syllables, or failing to segment the speech stream into words.

examples.note: Connected speech in Norwegian often merges 'jeg er' into a single sound.
Negation particles are often weakly stressed and easily missed.

comprehension.label: Reading parsing
comprehension.description: The learner can read the individual words but fails to correctly parse the sentence structure — typically misidentifying the verb, subject, or clause boundaries.

examples.wrong: Parsed 'Etter middagen gikk vi hjem' with 'middagen' as subject
examples.correct: 'vi' is the subject; 'etter middagen' is a fronted adverbial
examples.note: V2 inversion makes subject identification harder when adverbials are fronted.

comprehension.label: Meaning misunderstood
comprehension.description: The learner correctly reads or hears the words but arrives at an incorrect interpretation of the overall meaning — often due to false friends, missed context, or pragmatic gaps.

examples.wrong: Interpreted 'Kan du ikke hjelpe meg?' as a refusal
examples.correct: It is a request for help (negative question).
examples.note: Norwegian negative questions are typically polite requests, not rhetorical challenges.
